=== SITE_importance-of-dpr-reports-why-it-is-essential-for-successful-completion-of-the-project_ ===
Importance of DPR in Construction Projects Skip to content Features Pre-Construction Planning Sales Management Design Management Budgeting Project Execution Progress Tracking Quality Management Procurement Management Production Management Resource Managment Labour Management Subcon Management Asset &#038; Equipment Material Management Finance &#038; Controls Project P&#038;L Vendor Billing Client Invoicing Reports &#038; Analytics Solutions Builders &#038; Developers Interior Companies Infra Companies Civil Contractors EPC Companies Resources About Case Studies Whatâ€™s New Feature Comparisons Construction Calculators Construction Terms Pricing Contact Us Get Product Demo Contact Form Demo (#7) Book Free 10 Min Demo Fill the form to see the product live Trusted by 10,000+ Construction companies â­â­â­â­â­ Name Phone/Mobile Company Name Designation - Select - Business Owner/Founder/CXO Contractor Architect / Interior Designer Project Manager Labour Contractor Site Manager / Supervisor Planning Manager Site Engineer Accountant Email Submit Form Features Pre-Construction Planning Sales Management Design Management Budgeting Project Execution Progress Tracking Quality Management Procurement Management Production Management Resource Managment Labour Management Subcon Management Asset &#038; Equipment Material Management Finance &#038; Controls Project P&#038;L Vendor Billing Client Invoicing Reports &#038; Analytics Solutions Builders &#038; Developers Interior Companies Infra Companies Civil Contractors EPC Companies Resources About Case Studies Whatâ€™s New Feature Comparisons Construction Calculators Construction Terms Pricing Contact Us Get Product Demo Menu In Blog Quick Guides By SiteFlow Editorial Team Published On July 10, 2022 Updated On June 3, 2026 Importance of DPR in Construction Projects Home Blog Importance of DPR in Construction Projects What is DPR in Construction Projects? DPR stands for &#8220;Detailed Project Report,&#8221; These reports are created after the planning & designing phase of the project. DPRs are in-depth project reports which contain critical information about the project, such as information about types of equipment, materials, essential tasks, and other resources. In this article, we will read about the importance of DPR reports in a construction project. Table of Contents Toggle What is DPR in Construction Projects? Components of Ideal DPR Importance of DPR Conclusion The importance of DPR can be traced by the fact that these reports contain important pieces of information about the tasks that are necessary for the completion of the project. The operational and implementation process begins after the examination of the DPRs. It is also called the &#8220;ultimate blueprint&#8221; of the project. Components of Ideal DPR Brief description of the project. Description of the abilities and experience of the crew, teams, and other subcontractors. Details about the project financing and funding sources. Approval certificates and necessary permits from the government. Requirements and details of the raw materials. Details and requirements about project security. Information about the equipment renting and purchasing. Importance of DPR A construction project is called successful when you execute it in the given time period and also secure your profit margin easily. Let&#8217;s understand how a DPR helps in achieving these two goals. Easy Budget Management Handling the project budget is a very crucial task because this determines your profit in a construction project. As there are soo many components involved in a construction project such as materials, labor salaries, machinery, team allowances, etc. Planning the budget becomes a very tough task. This is where DPR helps and assists you in effectively planning the budget. With the help of DPR, you can be transparent about the budget with all the concerned parties so that you don&#8217;t exceed the budget and secure your profit margin. Timely Execution No matter how closely you pay attention to the details and issues of the projects, potential risks such as materials shortage, labor unavailability, and machinery failure can occur. As a result, these risks can delay the project. With DPR, you can plan and assess these potential risks at the early stages and try to minimize and eliminate them so that you can smoothly run site operations and ensure that all the project works are completed within the stipulated period. Conclusion A DPR has innumerable benefits. That&#8217;s why it is an essential part of the project. A contractor should always try and implement the best practices of creating and maintaining smart and efficient DPRs for a better success rate in project completion. What is a GPS Attendance Management System? A GPS Attendance Management System uses a GPS tracker for employees or an employee GPS tracking app to record when and where workers check in and out. On construction sites this ensures accurate location tracking and real work-site attendance. 2. How does a gps tracker for employees improve attendance accuracy? A GPS tracker for employees removes guesswork and manual logs. It captures real-time location and timestamped check-in/out, which avoids errors that come with manual tracking or paper-based attendance. 3. Can this system handle multiple construction sites at once? Yes. With an employee GPS tracking app, you can manage attendance across many jobsites. That makes it easy for contractors overseeing several projects to track labour at different locations. 4. Will using an employee gps tracking app boost on-site productivity? Yes. By ensuring workers mark attendance only when physically at the site, and by giving supervisors real-time location tracking, the app promotes discipline and accountability â€” which improves productivity on-site. 5. Does this solution help reduce payroll errors and disputes? Yes. Because attendance and hours are recorded automatically and accurately via gps tracker for employees, payroll calculations become more reliable â€” reducing chances of incorrect wages or disputes over attendance. 6. Is the system easy for labourers with minimal tech knowledge to use? Yes. The employee GPS tracking app is designed for simplicity. Workers only need to tap to check in or out; the app handles location tracking and data logging â€” no manual registers required. Importance of DPR in Construction Projects Skip to content Features Pre-Construction Planning Sales Management Design Management Budgeting Project Execution Progress Tracking Quality Management Procurement Management Production Management Resource Managment Labour Management Subcon Management Asset &#038; Equipment Material Management Finance &#038; Controls Project P&#038;L Vendor Billing Client Invoicing Reports &#038; Analytics Solutions Builders &#038; Developers Interior Companies Infra Companies Civil Contractors EPC Companies Resources About Case Studies Whatâ€™s New Feature Comparisons Construction Calculators Construction Terms Pricing Contact Us Get Product Demo Contact Form Demo (#7) Book Free 10 Min Demo Fill the form to see the product live Trusted by 10,000+ Construction companies â­â­â­â­â­ Name Phone/Mobile Company Name Designation - Select - Business Owner/Founder/CXO Contractor Architect / Interior Designer Project Manager Labour Contractor Site Manager / Supervisor Planning Manager Site Engineer Accountant Email Submit Form Features Pre-Construction Planning Sales Management Design Management Budgeting Project Execution Progress Tracking Quality Management Procurement Management Production Management Resource Managment Labour Management Subcon Management Asset &#038; Equipment Material Management Finance &#038; Controls Project P&#038;L Vendor Billing Client Invoicing Reports &#038; Analytics Solutions Builders &#038; Developers Interior Companies Infra Companies Civil Contractors EPC Companies Resources About Case Studies Whatâ€™s New Feature Comparisons Construction Calculators Construction Terms Pricing Contact Us Get Product Demo Menu In Blog Quick Guides By SiteFlow Editorial Team Published On July 10, 2022 Updated On June 3, 2026 Importance of DPR in Construction Projects Home Blog Importance of DPR in Construction Projects What is DPR in Construction Projects? DPR stands for &#8220;Detailed Project Report,&#8221; These reports are created after the planning & designing phase of the project. DPRs are in-depth project reports which contain critical information about the project, such as information about types of equipment, materials, essential tasks, and other resources. In this article, we will read about the importance of DPR reports in a construction project. Table of Contents Toggle What is DPR in Construction Projects? Components of Ideal DPR Importance of DPR Conclusion The importance of DPR can be traced by the fact that these reports contain important pieces of information about the tasks that are necessary for the completion of the project. The operational and implementation process begins after the examination of the DPRs. It is also called the &#8220;ultimate blueprint&#8221; of the project. Components of Ideal DPR Brief description of the project. Description of the abilities and experience of the crew, teams, and other subcontractors. Details about the project financing and funding sources. Approval certificates and necessary permits from the government. Requirements and details of the raw materials. Details and requirements about project security. Information about the equipment renting and purchasing. Importance of DPR A construction project is called successful when you execute it in the given time period and also secure your profit margin easily. Let&#8217;s understand how a DPR helps in achieving these two goals. Easy Budget Management Handling the project budget is a very crucial task because this determines your profit in a construction project. As there are soo many components involved in a construction project such as materials, labor salaries, machinery, team allowances, etc. Planning the budget becomes a very tough task. This is where DPR helps and assists you in effectively planning the budget. With the help of DPR, you can be transparent about the budget with all the concerned parties so that you don&#8217;t exceed the budget and secure your profit margin. Timely Execution No matter how closely you pay attention to the details and issues of the projects, potential risks such as materials shortage, labor unavailability, and machinery failure can occur. As a result, these risks can delay the project. With DPR, you can plan and assess these potential risks at the early stages and try to minimize and eliminate them so that you can smoothly run site operations and ensure that all the project works are completed within the stipulated period. Conclusion A DPR has innumerable benefits. That&#8217;s why it is an essential part of the project. A contractor should always try and implement the best practices of creating and maintaining smart and efficient DPRs for a better success rate in project completion. Our platform integrates with accounting, communication and productivity tools like Tally, Zoho Books, SAP, WhatsApp and Excel. FAQs What is Construction Management Software? Construction Management Software (CMS) is a digital platform designed to plan, execute, and monitor construction projects from start to finish. It manages workflows, schedules, resources, and communication. However, a true Construction ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning) goes further. Onsite Teams is a specialized ERP that focuses on cost control by integrating financial data with site activity. This means we track every material indent, labor hour, and procurement cost in real-time a level of detail generic CMS and PM tools cannot match. What is the best construction management software (CMS) in India? It offers real-time site updates through a mobile app and advanced planning through a desktop ERP, ensuring faster adoption, easy usability, and end-to-end control of your projects. Conclusion Want to manage your multiple construction projects at one place? FAQs What Real Estate Execution Management Means for Teams Today? Managing a real estate project is no longer limited to scheduling tasks. It involves continuous coordination between multiple moving parts: design changes and site execution contractors, subcontractors, and internal teams daily progress and financial commitments approvals, documentation, and compliance When all of these tasks are handled separately or single-handedly, teams can only rely either on assumptions or follow-ups. But as the size of project becomes bigger, the methods should become more reliable and systematic, so that the tasks that has been being handled in isolation does not become risky and inefficient. A project management system is made to make site execution easy and doable so that the decisions that has been made in executing a project stay grounded in what actually is happening and remains less on guesswork. What Is Real Estate Execution Software? You can think of it as your project&#8217;s office that you can carry with you. It combines planning, scheduling, budgeting, communication, approvals, and reporting into one tool. This means that developers and contractors will have fewer misunderstandings, less paperwork, and a lot more faith that things are going as planned. You can see everything in one dashboard, anytime and wherever, instead of having to chase updates through calls and emails. Where Traditional Workflows Break Down? Real Estate Execution with traditional methods are always dependent on tools like emails, Excel, WhatsApp, or phone calls. These different tools do not become very supportive for day-to-day management. These tools were never designed for construction in the first place. Many teams still rely on generic spreadsheets rather than specialized project planning software, leading to misalignment and delays. Common issues include: progress updates reaching decision-makers too late multiple versions of drawings and documents circulating approvals getting stuck because context is missing budgets reviewed after costs are already incurred billing delayed due to incomplete or unreliable records These problems donâ€™t arise overnight. They build quietly as projects move forward without a single source of truth. Without dedicated project tracking software, critical updates can slip through the cracks and cause costly project setbacks. How Onsite Helps in Real Estate Execution? Plan & Schedule Projects with Ease A top advantage of Real Estate Execution management software is its ability to structure complex timelines and dependencies. Onsite helps you set precise deadlines, assign tasks, and see how things are going in real time. You can see every milestone, so there are no surprises. Keep Budgets & Expenses Under Control Real estate has small profit margins. Onsite allows you to watch expenses as they happen, compare against estimates instantly, and eliminate overruns before they eat into earnings. With project management platforms designed for real estate, teams gain clear oversight of budgets , schedules, and deliverables. Track Materials & Billing Transparently Projects often stop because of unbilled entries and a lack of materials. With Onsite, you can keep an eye on purchases right from the site, watch the movement of materials, and keep a complete record of things that haven&#8217;t been invoiced yet, so you don&#8217;t miss anything. Centralized Communication & Documentation All of your drawings, contracts, approvals, payslips, invoices , bills, and changes are in one safe place. Teams and consultants don&#8217;t have to hop between applications or look through emails. Manage Retention & Compliance Smoothly Onsite gives you a specific area for your retention money, compliance papers, and audit files. Everything keeps in order and is ready when needed. Teams adopting project management applications see measurable gains in coordination and delivery. Real-Time Visibility Anyone, from project managers on site to senior management in the office, may log in and monitor the status of a project in real time. This cuts down on unnecessary site visits, speeds up decisions, and makes it obvious who is responsible. Many modern tools include project timeline software to visualize progress and communicate it to stakeholders. A Practical View of Impact When real estate teams switch to structured project workflows, the change is clear in their daily work right away. Updates to the site come on schedule and in a consistent way. Approval cycles shorten because the information needed to decide is already there. Teams work together better without having to have extra meetings or follow-ups. It&#8217;s easier to defend billing when there are detailed records of how it was done. Management gets visibility into progress without having to micromanage each site. Adding more steps to the procedure won&#8217;t help you get these benefits. They emerge from better rules about how information is gathered, shared, and used throughout the project. What AI Data Shows About Daily Progress Tracking in Construction? Smart Payroll Generation Once attendance is recorded and rates are fed in, Onsite calculates wages automatically according to the hours a worker has worked. Transparent Pay Slip Sharing Your pay slip generation is just a click away with Onsite. Now you do not need to manually make a pay slip. On the basis of worker&#8217;s data and their shift, Onsite will calculate their wage and will make a pay slip instantly. It saves a lot of time and makes the work easier. This will also help in bringing productivity to your backhand staff. Lap lengths and stirrup deductions are calculated per IS standards. Are the case study results real? + Yes. No sign-up, no login, no limit on how many times you use them. All calculators on this page are built and maintained by Onsite and are available to anyone working in Indian construction. Which IS code standards do the steel calculators follow? + All steel calculators use IS 456:2000 formulas. The unit weight formula is D&#178; &#247; 163 (kg/m), where D is bar diameter in mm. Lap lengths are calculated as 50D for columns. Stirrup cutting lengths account for 90&#176; and 135&#176; bend deductions as per IS standards. The Bar Bending Schedule calculator also follows SP 34 guidelines for shape codes. What is the difference between the one-way slab and two-way slab calculators? + A one-way slab (Ly/Lx greater than 2) carries load in the shorter direction only. Main bars run along the shorter span; distribution bars run in the longer direction. A two-way slab (Ly/Lx of 2 or less) carries load in both directions, so main reinforcement is needed along both Lx and Ly. Use the one-way calculator for beams spanning in one direction and the two-way for panels supported on all four sides. Does the brick calculator account for mortar and wastage? + Yes. The brick calculator adds a 10 mm mortar joint to both length and height when counting bricks. It also calculates wet and dry mortar volume separately, then breaks that down into cement bags and sand quantity using a 1:6 mix ratio. Wastage is adjustable â€” the default is 10%, which covers standard site cutting. Decorative or diagonal brickwork should use 15 to 20%. How accurate are the paint quantity estimates? + The paint calculator uses standard coverage rates: putty at 1.3 kg per m&#178; for two coats, primer at 9 L per m&#178; for one coat, and finish paint at 6.5 L per m&#178; for two coats. These match the coverage figures used by most Indian paint brands on their technical data sheets. Results will be close to actual consumption for smooth plastered surfaces. Textured or rough surfaces will use more â€” add 10 to 15% in those cases. What does the Bar Bending Schedule calculator produce? + It generates a complete BBS table with bar mark, bar type, diameter, shape code, cutting length, number of bars, and total weight per bar mark. The output follows the format used on most Indian construction sites and can be exported for procurement or site use. The AI layer helps verify shape codes and flag common input errors before you finalise the schedule. Can I use these calculators on mobile? + Yes. All calculators are mobile-first and work on any browser without downloading an app. Site engineers and supervisors can use them directly from the field on a phone. Results load instantly without any page reload. How is Onsite ERP different from these free calculators? + These calculators give you a number. Understanding these derivations helps engineers verify their schedules and catch errors before steel reaches the fabrication yard. 1 Steel Density Density of steel = 7850 kg/mÂ³ (IS 800 standard value) 2 Bar Cross-section Area = (Ï€/4) Ã— DÂ² D in metres = Ï€ Ã— DÂ²(mm) / (4 Ã— 10â¶) 3 Weight per Metre W = Area Ã— density = (Ï€/4) Ã— (D/1000)Â² Ã— 7850 per metre of bar 4 Simplify Ï€ Ã— 7850 / (4 Ã— 10â¶) â‰ˆ 1/162 âˆ´ W = DÂ² Ã· 162 kg/m DÂ²/162 â€” Universal Steel Weight Formula Weight (kg/m) = DÂ² Ã· 162 D = diameter in mm | Total weight = (DÂ² Ã· 162) Ã— Total Length (m) Worked Example â€” Rectangular Stirrup (IS 2502) Given: Rectangular stirrup, A = 300 mm, B = 450 mm, dia = 10 mm, IS 2502 (90Â° hook), cover = 25 mm Bend deduction (4 Ã— 90Â° corners): 4 Ã— 2d = 4 Ã— 20 = 80 mm â†’ 8d = 80 mm Hook allowance (IS 2502, both ends): 2 Ã— 9d = 2 Ã— 90 = 180 mm Perimeter: 2 Ã— (300 + 450) = 1500 mm Cover deduction: 2 Ã— 25 = 50 mm Cutting length = 1500 âˆ’ 80 + 180 âˆ’ 50 = 1550 mm = 1.550 m Weight per bar = 1.550 Ã— (10Â² Ã· 162) = 1.550 Ã— 0.617 = 0.957 kg Steel Bar Weight Chart â€” 6mm to 32mm (IS 1786) Reference table for TMT bar weight per metre and per standard 12 m length. Note the number of members (e.g., number of beams) and bars per member. 2 Set Global Parameters Decide on the hook standard (IS 2502 for general use, IS 13920 for seismic zones IIIâ€“V), nominal cover per IS 456, standard bar length (12 m), and material rate per kg for cost estimation. 3 Calculate Cutting Lengths Apply the appropriate IS 2502 formula for each bar shape. Deduct 2 Ã— cover from the overall dimension. Add hook allowances. Deduct bend allowances. Check each bar mark independently. 4 Calculate Weight Total length = cutting length Ã— no. of bars Ã— no. of members + lap lengths. Weight (kg) = total length Ã— DÂ²/162. Bars to order = âŒˆtotal length Ã· standard bar lengthâŒ‰. 5 Review and Submit Cross-check with the abstract of reinforcement in the BOQ. Add 3â€“5% wastage for cutting losses. Export to Excel or print for site and procurement teams. Get sign-off from the structural engineer. Wastage Guidelines (IS 456) Standard reinforcement work: 3â€“5% Complex shapes / many short bars: 5â€“8% Site cutting (no pre-fabrication): 8â€“10% Add extra for couplers or threaded ends: +1â€“2% Default in this calculator: 5% IS Codes Reference IS 2502:1963 â€” Code of practice for bending and fixing of bars for concrete reinforcement IS 13920:2016 â€” Ductile detailing for seismic resistance IS 456:2000 â€” Plain and reinforced concrete (code of practice) IS 1786:2008 â€” High strength deformed steel bars (TMT) SP 34:1987 â€” Handbook on concrete reinforcement and detailing Frequently Asked Questions What is a Bar Bending Schedule (BBS)? + A Bar Bending Schedule (BBS) is a detailed document prepared by structural engineers that lists every reinforcement bar in a concrete structure with its bar mark, shape, diameter, cutting length, number of bars, and total weight. It is used for material procurement, cutting and bending at the fabrication yard, and billing purposes. A well-prepared BBS reduces steel wastage, speeds up site execution, and provides a clear audit trail for quantity surveyors. How is cutting length calculated for a stirrup? + For a rectangular stirrup of dimensions A Ã— B: Cutting Length = 2(A + B) âˆ’ 8d + Hook Allowance âˆ’ 2 Ã— Cover The âˆ’8d accounts for four 90Â° bend deductions (2d each corner). IS 2502 uses 90Â° hooks with allowance 2 Ã— 9d = 18d added. IS 13920 uses 135Â° seismic hooks with allowance 2 Ã— 12d = 24d added. Cover is deducted from both ends. For a 300Ã—450 mm stirrup in 10 mm dia with IS 2502 and 25 mm cover: CL = 2(300+450) âˆ’ 80 + 180 âˆ’ 50 = 1550 mm. What is the DÂ²/162 formula? + The DÂ²/162 formula calculates the weight of a steel bar in kg per metre of length. It is derived from first principles: Weight per metre = (Ï€/4) Ã— (D/1000)Â² Ã— 7850 kg/mÂ³ = Ï€ Ã— 7850 / (4 Ã— 10â¶) Ã— DÂ² â‰ˆ DÂ² / 162 kg/m where D is the bar diameter in mm and 7850 kg/mÂ³ is the density of steel per IS 800. Total weight = (DÂ²/162) Ã— Total Length in metres. What is the difference between IS 2502 and IS 13920 hooks? + IS 2502 specifies standard 90Â° hooks with a minimum straight extension of 4d beyond the bend radius, giving a total hook allowance of approximately 9d. IS 13920 (seismic detailing) requires 135Â° hooks with a minimum straight extension of 10d beyond the bend, giving a total hook allowance of 12d. The 135Â° hook provides superior anchorage and confinement in earthquake-resistant joints. Use IS 13920 hooks for all stirrups and ties in structures located in seismic zones III, IV and V as per IS 1893. How much wastage should I add for steel? + A wastage allowance of 3â€“5% is standard for straightforward reinforcement work where bars are pre-fabricated off-site and the BBS is well-prepared. Increase to 5â€“8% for complex structures with many different bar lengths, short offcuts, or when cutting is done on-site. The default in this calculator is 5%, which is widely accepted for estimation and tendering purposes in India. Note that the wastage % applies to the total weight after all cutting lengths are calculated. What is the standard length of steel bars in India? + Steel reinforcement bars (TMT Fe 500 / Fe 500D) are supplied in standard lengths of 12 metres in India, as per IS 1786:2008. Some suppliers also offer 9 m and 11.5 m lengths for smaller diameters (6â€“10 mm) for easier transportation. The Bars to Order column in this calculator divides the total required length by the standard bar length (default 12 m) and rounds up to the nearest whole number. You can change the standard bar length in the Settings Bar above to match your supplier&#8217;s stock length. How do I calculate laps in a BBS? + Lap length as per IS 456:2000 is typically 50 times the bar diameter (50d) for tension laps in Fe 500 steel in M20 concrete. This is the minimum â€” check the structural drawing notes for the specified lap length as it can be 40d, 50d or 60d depending on concrete grade and bar position. In this calculator, enter the number of laps per bar in the No. Laps column. The lap addition = No. of Laps Ã— 50d per bar, which is added to the cutting length before multiplying by the number of bars and members. What is cover and why is it deducted in BBS? + Cover (or clear cover) is the distance between the outer face of concrete and the nearest surface of a reinforcement bar. It protects steel from corrosion, fire, and carbonation. In a BBS, cover is deducted from both ends of the bar (i.e., 2 Ã— cover is subtracted from the overall bar dimension) to find the net cutting length. Typical minimum covers per IS 456: columns and beams = 25â€“40 mm; slabs = 20â€“25 mm; footings = 40â€“50 mm; members exposed to soil or water = 50 mm. The cover setting in the calculator&#8217;s Settings Bar applies this deduction to all rows. BBS kaise banate hain? (Hindi) + BBS (Bar Bending Schedule) banane ke liye yeh steps follow karein: 1. Drawing padhein â€” Structural drawing se sabhi bar marks, shape, diameter aur dimensions note karein. 2. Global settings set karein â€” Hook standard (IS 2502 ya IS 13920), cover (mm), standard bar length (12 m) aur rate (â‚¹/kg) set karein. 3. Cutting length calculate karein â€” Har bar ke liye IS 2502 ka formula use karein. Cover deduct karein, hook allowance add karein, bend deduction hatayein. 4. Total length aur weight nikaalein â€” Total length = CL Ã— no. of bars Ã— no. of members. Weight = Total length Ã— DÂ²/162. 5. Export ya print karein â€” Excel export ya A4 print option use karein site aur procurement ke liye. Upar diye calculator mein yeh sab automatic calculate hota hai â€” sirf dimensions enter karein. Can this BBS calculator be used for UAE/international projects? + Yes. The DÂ²/162 weight formula is universal and matches values in BS 8666 and ACI 318 reference tables. For UAE/GCC projects: (1) Use the IS 13920 hook setting (135Â°) which matches ACI 318-19 seismic hook requirements. (2) Adjust nominal cover to match project specification (BS 8500 exposure class or ACI 318 requirements). (3) Change the standard bar length from 12 m to 6 m (common stock length in UAE) or 9 m as required. The Excel export includes all project header information suitable for submission to MEP contractors and quantity surveying teams. Mortar joint is typically 10 mm 4 Set wastage â€” 10% is standard; use 15â€“20% for complex shapes with lots of cuts 5 Add price per brick (optional) to instantly see total material cost The Formula Brick Calculation Formula for Wall The standard brickwork formula divides wall area by the face area of a single brick including its mortar joint. Here&#8217;s how to apply it step by step. Brick Calculation Formula Bricks = (L &times; H) &divide; ((l + t) &times; (h + t)) L = wall length &bull; H = wall height &bull; l = brick length &bull; h = brick height &bull; t = mortar joint. Multiply by 2 for double-leaf (9-inch) walls. 1 Measure wall area Multiply wall length Ã— height. Subtract area of any doors or windows. 2 Choose brick size Standard Indian brick: 190 &times; 90 mm face. UK: 215 &times; 65 mm. US: 203 &times; 95 mm. 3 Add mortar joint Standard mortar joint is 10 mm. Add t to both brick length and height before dividing. 4 Add wastage 10% for straight walls. 15% with openings. Up to 20% for complex cuts. ðŸ“ Worked Example â€” 5 m &times; 3 m wall, Standard Indian Brick (190 &times; 90 &times; 90 mm), 10 mm mortar, single leaf Wall area = 5.0 m &times; 3.0 m = 15.00 mÂ² Brick face (with mortar) = (190+10) mm &times; (90+10) mm = 200 &times; 100 = 0.0200 mÂ² Net bricks needed = 15.00 &divide; 0.0200 = 750 bricks Wastage (10%) = 750 &times; 0.10 = 75 bricks Total to order = 750 + 75 = 825 bricks Quick Reference How Many Bricks Per Square Metre by Wall Thickness? What is the formula to calculate the number of bricks in a wall? + The standard brick calculation formula is: Bricks = (Wall Length &times; Wall Height) &divide; ((Brick Length + Mortar) &times; (Brick Height + Mortar)) For a 5 m &times; 3 m wall using Indian standard bricks (190 &times; 90 mm) with 10 mm mortar: Bricks = (5 &times; 3) &divide; (0.200 &times; 0.100) = 750 bricks Add 10% wastage â†’ order 825 bricks . For a 9-inch double-leaf wall, multiply by 2 â†’ 1,650 bricks . How many bricks per square metre for a 9-inch wall? + A 9-inch (230 mm) double-leaf wall requires 100 bricks per mÂ² using standard Indian bricks (190 &times; 90 &times; 90 mm) with a 10 mm mortar joint. In sq ft: approximately 9.3 bricks per sq ft . Including 10% wastage, order 110 bricks per mÂ² . For a 5 m &times; 3 m wall (15 mÂ²): order 1,650 bricks total. How many bricks are required for a 4.5-inch wall per square metre? + A 4.5-inch (half-brick, single-leaf) wall requires 50 bricks per mÂ² â€” approximately 4.6 bricks per sq ft . Including 10% wastage, order 55 bricks per mÂ² . A 5 m &times; 3 m wall = 15 mÂ² â†’ order 825 bricks . What is the standard mortar joint thickness for brickwork? + The standard mortar joint thickness is 10 mm (approx 3/8 inch) for both horizontal bed joints and vertical perpend joints per IS 1905 . Joints below 3 mm are used for thin-joint / AAC block work. Joints above 15 mm reduce structural strength and should be avoided. How do you calculate bricks for a boundary wall? + Step-by-step boundary wall calculation: Measure total perimeter length in metres Multiply by wall height to get total area (mÂ²) For a 4.5&#8243; wall: area &times; 50 | For a 9&#8243; wall: area &times; 100 Add 10â€“15% for wastage and corner cuts Example: 40 m perimeter &times; 2.5 m high = 100 mÂ². 9&#8243; wall: 100 &times; 100 + 10% = 11,000 bricks . How many bricks are needed to build a house? + For a 1,000 sq ft single-storey house in India: 4.5-inch walls: approximately 8,000â€“10,000 bricks 9-inch walls: approximately 14,000â€“18,000 bricks A double-storey house typically requires 25,000â€“40,000 bricks. Use the calculator above with your actual wall measurements for a precise estimate. What wastage factor should I use for brickwork? + 5â€“10% â€” Simple straight wall, no openings 10â€“15% â€” Wall with doors, windows, standard corners 15â€“20% â€” Complex shapes, arches, curved walls 20%+ â€” Intricate decorative or diagonal brickwork Always round up to the nearest full delivery pack when ordering. à¤ˆà¤‚à¤Ÿ à¤¨à¤¿à¤•à¤¾à¤²à¤¨à¥‡ à¤•à¤¾ à¤«à¤¾à¤°à¥à¤®à¥‚à¤²à¤¾ à¤•à¥à¤¯à¤¾ à¤¹à¥ˆ? Led by General Manager Eng. Ahmed Mokhtar, the company delivers cast-in-place reinforced concrete pools, overflow pools, architectural fountains, steam rooms, saunas, and full-service pool maintenance to luxury villa owners, hotels, and commercial clients across Qatar and the Middle East. With a team of 18 plus specialists spanning MEP, procurement, project management, design drafting, and maintenance, Hydro Flow Qatar Pools manages multiple concurrent projects where professional client documentation and real-time reporting are as important to client satisfaction as the quality of construction itself. CLIENT NAME Hydro Flow Qatar Pools CLIENT LOCATION Doha, Qatar CATEGORY Luxury Pool Construction & Water Features The Challenges Hydro Flow Qatar Pools Faced Before Onsite As Hydro Flow Qatar Pools grew its portfolio of concurrent luxury projects across Qatar, the manual systems the team relied on for reporting and client communication began creating operational bottlenecks that directly affected the professional experience clients expected from a company of Hydro Flow Qatar&#8217;s standing. 01 Manual Report Compilation Was Consuming Team Time Across Every Active Project Every client update at Hydro Flow Qatar Pools required someone to manually gather progress photographs from WhatsApp, pull cost and activity data from separate spreadsheets, format everything into a presentable document, and distribute it to the client. With a team managing multiple concurrent pool and water feature projects simultaneously across Qatar, this manual compilation cycle repeated across every project every reporting period. The time consumed by report preparation pulled MEP and project management team members away from technical work and site supervision that needed their full attention. 02 Sharing Documents With Clients and Vendors Involved Multiple Manual Steps Getting a project report, procurement document, or progress update to a luxury villa client or equipment vendor required locating the right file, converting or formatting it appropriately, attaching it to an email or WhatsApp message, and following up manually to confirm receipt. For a company serving high-expectation clients who commission projects worth significant investment, this informal distribution process produced documents that were inconsistent in format, frequently outdated by the time they arrived, and lacking the professional presentation that Hydro Flow Qatar Pools&#8217; market position demanded. 03 Site Records and Office Records Never Reflected the Same Project Reality Progress information at active Hydro Flow Qatar sites existed in multiple disconnected versions. Site supervisors tracked work informally through WhatsApp photographs and verbal updates. The procurement and finance team worked from purchase orders and invoices. Project managers reconciled the two manually at reporting time. By the time a consolidated report reached a client, the numbers and progress status it contained reflected a version of the project that was already days behind actual site reality. Discrepancies between what reports said and what clients observed at site visits created uncomfortable conversations that manual reconciliation could not prevent. How Onsite Solved It? Hydro Flow Qatar Pools implemented Onsite across active projects. The implementation focused on three capabilities directly connected to their reporting, communication, and data consistency challenges. Hydro Flow Qatar Pools &middot; How Onsite Solved It One version. Across site, office, and client. On luxury pool construction in Qatar, the report a client receives has to match what is actually happening on site. Hydro Flow Qatar Pools replaced hours of manual compilation with structured PDF reports generated directly from live project data &mdash; one source of truth flowing through site teams, project managers, and client-facing documentation alike. 01 Automated PDF Report Generation from Live Project Data Project reports at Hydro Flow Qatar Pools stopped being a manual compilation task once Onsite went live. Site teams updated daily progress through the Onsite mobile app with geo-tagged photographs and activity completion entries. Structured PDF reports generated automatically from that verified data, covering activity status, site photography with timestamps, procurement progress, and cost tracking in a consistent professional format across every project. Ltd. is an infrastructure and road construction contractor operating across multiple states in North India, associated with Deepak Earth Movers which brings over 20 years of market presence to the business. Founded by Mr. SF Chougule, the company manages earthwork, road construction, and civil infrastructure projects with a workforce of 50 to 100 labourers deployed across concurrent sites at any given time. In an industry where labour costs represent the single largest project expense, the gap between paper attendance and actual site presence determines whether projects remain profitable or absorb silent losses every pay cycle. CLIENT NAME SFC Infratech Pvt. Ltd. CLIENT LOCATION North India CATEGORY Infrastructure and Road Construction The Challenges SFC Infratech Faced Before Onsite As Reidius Infra grew from managing a handful of projects to running multiple concurrent sites across Jaipur, the informal tools the team relied on began creating operational failures that directly affected client relationships and delivery commitments. 01 Paper Registers With No Verification Created Systematic Payroll Errors Attendance at every SFC Infratech site ran on paper registers maintained by site supervisors. Workers marked attendance manually with no verification that the person present matched the record being created. Registers were submitted to the head office at the end of each week or pay cycle. By the time payroll teams received and processed them, errors from misrecorded attendance, duplicate entries, and absent workers marked as present had already compounded across the full workforce with no mechanism to identify which entries were accurate. 02 Ghost Worker Entries Were Invisible to Management Without identity verification at the point of check-in, workers listed on payroll who were not present on site had no structural barrier preventing their entries from accumulating across pay cycles. Site-level attendance was entirely dependent on supervisor honesty and accuracy. Management at the head office had no visibility into who was physically present at any given site on any given day. Labour costs on individual projects were impossible to verify against actual deployment, and headcount overstatement was undetectable until it surfaced through financial reconciliation. 03 Multi-Site Payroll Consolidation Consumed Days Every Cycle SFC Infratech&#8217;s expansion across multiple concurrent North India sites meant payroll was compiled separately at each location by different supervisors using different paper formats. Consolidating payroll required manual gathering of registers from every site, format reconciliation, discrepancy identification, and full workforce wage calculation before any payments could be processed. This consumed three or more days every pay cycle and still produced errors. Workers deployed across more than one site during a pay period created reconciliation problems that paper systems could not resolve cleanly. How Onsite Solved It? SFC Infratech implemented Onsite across all active sites. The implementation focused on four capabilities directly connected to their labour management and payroll challenges. SFC Infratech &middot; How Onsite Solved It No paper. No proxy. No disputes. The manual payroll consolidation that used to take three days or more every pay period now runs automatically from verified face-recognition attendance across every SFC Infratech site. Paper registers, proxy check-ins, and payment disputes all closed out through the same pipeline. 01 Geo-Tagged Attendance and Face Recognition Replaced Paper Registers Workers at every SFC Infratech site began marking attendance through the Onsite mobile app using face recognition that verified identity combined with GPS confirmation that verified physical presence at the correct site. Both verifications had to pass simultaneously for the attendance entry to record. Proxy attendance and ghost worker entries became structurally impossible because every record required verified identity and confirmed site location. Paper registers disappeared entirely from the attendance workflow. &#10003; Face recognition confirming verified worker identity at every check-in &#10003; GPS location proof confirming physical presence at the correct site &#10003; Proxy attendance and ghost worker entries structurally eliminated 02 Automated Payroll Calculation with Integrated Advance Tracking Once attendance was verified through Onsite, payroll calculations ran automatically from the system based on agreed wage rates, verified attendance records, approved advances, and applicable deductions. The manual consolidation process that previously consumed three or more days was replaced by automated calculations drawing from centralized verified data. Advances recorded throughout the pay period reflected automatically in final wage calculations, eliminating the separate advance register reconciliation that had been a recurring source of errors. &#10003; Wage calculations running automatically from verified attendance records &#10003; Advance and deduction tracking integrated within the same platform &#10003; Multi-site payroll consolidated without manual register collection 03 Instant Payslip Generation Eliminated Worker Disputes Every worker received a digital payslip showing the exact days attended, wage rate applied, advances deducted, and final payment amount generated automatically from the same verified data that drove the wage calculation. When workers questioned their payment, supervisors showed them the verified attendance record tied directly to the payslip calculation. Disputes dropped immediately because workers could see their own attendance history and confirm how wages were calculated rather than arguing against an opaque manual process. &#10003; Digital payslips generated automatically from verified attendance data &#10003; Complete wage calculation breakdown visible to workers and supervisors &#10003; Worker trust in payroll accuracy improved across all active sites The Results Within the first two months of Onsite implementation, measurable improvements appeared across payroll accuracy, labour cost control, and management visibility. Payroll errors reduced to near zero The structural elimination of proxy attendance and ghost worker entries through face recognition and GPS verification removed the primary sources of payroll error from the process entirely. Errors that previously entered through manual register compilation, inconsistent supervisor recording, and advance reconciliation failures could not occur within a system where attendance was verified at the point of entry and payroll calculated automatically from that verified data. SFC Infratech&#8217;s pay cycles shifted from error-prone processes requiring post-cycle correction to reliable processes producing accurate outputs the first time. 25% reduction in labour costs Eliminating ghost worker entries and proxy attendance removed workers who were being paid without verified presence on site. The 25% reduction in labour costs reflected the gap between what manual paper attendance was billing to projects and what verified face recognition attendance confirmed was the actual workforce present. For a company operating at INR 40 to 50 crore annual revenue with labour representing a significant share of project costs, the recovery from ghost worker elimination had direct impact on project profitability across all active sites. 3 days saved monthly on payroll processing The manual consolidation that required three or more days of head office effort every pay cycle to gather registers, reconcile formats, calculate wages, and resolve multi-site discrepancies was replaced entirely by automated calculations running from centralized verified data. Payroll teams that previously spent most of a work week on compilation work shifted to oversight and exception handling. The recovered time went toward higher-value finance and operations work rather than administrative reconciliation of paper records from multiple sites. Message from the Founder of SFC Infratech Customer Success Story &ldquo; I never imagined that my business would be digitized so easily. Before Onsite, managing attendance and payroll across our sites required constant follow-up with supervisors and still produced errors we could not fully explain. With Onsite, attendance is verified at the point of check-in and payroll calculates itself. Check Pricing FAQs What problems did SFC Infratech face before using Onsite? SFC Infratech managed workforce attendance across multiple North India sites through paper registers with no identity verification at the point of check-in. This created systematic payroll errors through proxy attendance, ghost worker entries, and manual compilation mistakes compounding across every pay cycle. Multi-site payroll consolidation required three or more days of manual head office work every cycle. Workers who disputed their wages had no way to verify their attendance history, creating recurring disputes that damaged site relationships and consumed supervisor time. Which Onsite modules did SFC Infratech implement? SFC Infratech implemented Onsite&#8217;s Labour Management module, specifically face recognition attendance with GPS verification, automated wage calculation, advance and deduction tracking, instant payslip generation, and multi-site labour visibility. The implementation focused entirely on the labour and payroll workflow because that was where SFC Infratech&#8217;s most significant operational and financial problems were concentrated. Labour productivity tracking connected to activity progress entries gave management operational intelligence across all active sites. How does face recognition attendance work for infrastructure construction sites? Workers mark attendance through the Onsite mobile app using face recognition that verifies the person checking in matches the registered worker profile, combined with GPS confirmation that verifies physical presence at the correct site. Both verifications must pass simultaneously for the attendance entry to record. This makes proxy attendance and ghost worker entries structurally impossible because no manual override exists. Attendance data flows directly into payroll calculations without any manual compilation step. How did Onsite reduce SFC Infratech&#8217;s labour costs by 25%? The 25% reduction came directly from eliminating ghost worker entries and proxy attendance that had been accumulating undetected under the paper register system. Ghost workers are workers listed on payroll who are not present on site but receive payment because manual systems have no verification mechanism to catch them. Face recognition attendance with GPS verification made ghost worker entries impossible because every attendance record required simultaneous identity verification and confirmed physical presence at the correct site. What results did SFC Infratech achieve with Onsite? SFC Infratech achieved three measurable outcomes. Payroll errors reduced to near zero as face recognition and GPS verification structurally eliminated the proxy attendance and ghost worker entries that had been the primary error sources. Labour costs reduced by approximately 25% as verified attendance replaced paper registers that had been overstating workforce headcount. Monthly payroll processing time reduced by three or more days as automated calculations replaced manual multi-site register consolidation, directly improving project profitability and management control. Is Onsite suitable for infrastructure and road construction companies? Yes. Onsite is specifically suited for infrastructure and road contractors managing large labour-intensive workforces across multiple concurrent sites. The face recognition and GPS attendance features address ghost worker and proxy attendance problems that are particularly acute in infrastructure construction where site supervisors operate with limited direct management oversight. Multi-site labour visibility and automated payroll calculation work across distributed deployments of any size. Companies managing 50 to 500 workers across three or more concurrent sites gain the most immediate impact from Onsite&#8217;s labour management capabilities. How long did Onsite implementation take for SFC Infratech? Onsite&#8217;s labour management module is designed for rapid deployment without lengthy technical implementation. The core attendance and payroll features are operational within days of account setup. Workers are registered with face recognition profiles during onboarding and supervisors use the mobile app which requires no technical expertise beyond basic smartphone usage. Most infrastructure companies see measurable improvements in payroll accuracy and processing time within the first complete pay cycle after go-live. Can Onsite scale with infrastructure companies managing a growing workforce? Yes. Adding a new site to Onsite takes minutes. New workers are registered with face recognition profiles through the mobile app without office involvement. Payroll calculations automatically include any new site and any new workers registered within the system. Management visibility scales automatically as new sites are added. SFC Infratech&#8217;s experience shows that companies can expand across additional concurrent sites while maintaining the same payroll accuracy and management control without adding administrative headcount. Onsite is a construction management software built to help construction companies run projects with clarity and control. As a complete construction ERP software, it connects site teams, project managers, procurement, and finance on one platform. By replacing scattered tools and manual tracking, Onsite construction software helps businesses save time, reduce cost leakages, and improve execution across every project. The result is a practical ERP software that supports real site operations, not just reports. Retention Money in Construction: Recovering Every Rupee Skip to content Features Pre-Construction Planning Sales Management Design Management Budgeting Project Execution Progress Tracking Quality Management Procurement Management Production Management Resource Managment Labour Management Subcon Management Asset &#038; Equipment Material Management Finance &#038; Controls Project P&#038;L Vendor Billing Client Invoicing Reports &#038; Analytics Solutions Builders &#038; Developers Interior Companies Infra Companies Civil Contractors EPC Companies Resources About Case Studies Whatâ€™s New Feature Comparisons Construction Calculators Construction Terms Pricing Contact Us Get Product Demo Contact Form Demo (#7) Book Free 10 Min Demo Fill the form to see the product live Trusted by 10,000+ Construction companies â­â­â­â­â­ Name Phone/Mobile Company Name Designation - Select - Business Owner/Founder/CXO Contractor Architect / Interior Designer Project Manager Labour Contractor Site Manager / Supervisor Planning Manager Site Engineer Accountant Email Submit Form Features Pre-Construction Planning Sales Management Design Management Budgeting Project Execution Progress Tracking Quality Management Procurement Management Production Management Resource Managment Labour Management Subcon Management Asset &#038; Equipment Material Management Finance &#038; Controls Project P&#038;L Vendor Billing Client Invoicing Reports &#038; Analytics Solutions Builders &#038; Developers Interior Companies Infra Companies Civil Contractors EPC Companies Resources About Case Studies Whatâ€™s New Feature Comparisons Construction Calculators Construction Terms Pricing Contact Us Get Product Demo Menu In Blog By SiteFlow Editorial Team Published On April 21, 2026 Updated On June 22, 2026 Retention Money in Construction: Recovering Every Rupee Home Blog Retention Money in Construction: Recovering Every Rupee A contractor in Hyderabad wraps up a Rs 2.8 crore commercial fit-out. Handover done, client satisfied, final RA bill cleared. But Rs 16 lakh in retention money that was deducted across 14 bills over 18 months never comes back. No dispute. No legal issue. Just no one following up, and a client who certainly did not volunteer the information. This happens across hundreds of projects every year. Retention money in construction is legally owed, contractually documented, and regularly lost because contractors fail to manage it as a separate process. The money does not disappear because of bad contracts. It disappears because of bad tracking. Table of Contents Toggle What Retention Money in Construction Means? Why Contractors Fail to Recover Retention Money in Construction? The Real Financial Cost of Untracked Retention Money in Construction How to Track and Recover Retention Systematically? Retention in Subcontractor Contracts: Where Back-to-Back Terms Break Down Make Retention Recovery a Non-Negotiable Closeout Step FAQs What Retention Money in Construction Means? The percentage of each certified payment that a client withholds from a contractor throughout the project is named as retention money. It is considered as a financial guarantee that the contractor completes the work and fixes the issues after handover. It is common to keep 5 to 10% of retention money from most of the Indian contractors. The money that has been held accumulates across every billing cycle and is released in two branches. The first tranche is released upon practical completion, when the client issues the completion certificate confirming all contracted work is substantially done. The second tranche is released after the defects liability period ends, which typically runs six to twelve months after handover. For government projects under CPWD, PWD, or NHAI frameworks, the retention clauses and release conditions follow standardized formats. Private contracts are far more variable, and that variability is exactly where the tracking problems begin. Why Contractors Fail to Recover Retention Money in Construction? The problem is really legal complexities. Every contractor is aware of the fact they are owed retention. The real problem is operational, and it comes with a pattern that repeats across every firm that manages construction projects through manual work, like Excel files or WhatsApp messages. The tracking gap at project closeout The biggest problem is irresponsibility. The project gets closed, and the team moves on to the next site. The account person files the billing history and marks the project complete. Nobody is responsible for keeping track of retention money. Nobody is assigned to monitor the defects liability period end date. The client who is holding the retention holds no obligation to remind anyone it is due. And this is how retention slips away. No formal claim submission It is commonly assumed that the client will process retention release on schedule. The problem is that they will not. Just a WhatsApp text of the retention balance is not going to push the client to make payment, which is why a formal return notice citing the contract clause, the completion date, and the retention amount should be given. A formal claim is needed to get the retention money. Team turnover The project manager who knew every billing detail has moved on. The person picking up the file months later has no context. Reconstructing the full retention balance from scattered emails and old Excel sheets takes hours that nobody makes time for. Poor documentation When all of the defects are corrected, the time comes to submit the second claim. The contractor needs the original contract, the completion certificate date, the per-bill retention deduction, and a log of any defects resolved during the DLP. When every single one of these things is not at one place, the claim gets delayed or dropped entirely. The Real Financial Cost of Untracked Retention Money in Construction Many contractors overlook retention money and underestimate the fact that it costs them. For example, a contractor is completing 8 projects per year on an average. At 5% retention on every project, there is a huge amount that is being retained. The average value of per project is rupees 1.5 crore. At 5% retention, each project generates rupees 7. lakh in withheld funds. In total of eight projects, that is costing 60 lakhs that has been sitting in various clients&#8217; pockets. If even the three of these retention claims are overlooked, that is costing rupees 22.5 lakh in money that a contractor never received. The secondary cost is working capital. Every rupee sitting uncollected with the previous client is a rupee not available to fund new projects, purchase materials, make subcontractor payments, and cover payrolls across multiple sites. Contractors end up blaming low margins or delayed payments on active projects, but one of the real problems is never receiving the retention money and overlooking the huge amount in a project. There is also a downstream effect on subcontractor relationships. Contractors who lose track of their own retention receivable always lose track of retention payable to subcontractors too. So it ruins the relationship of a principal contractor and subcontractor. It creates delayed payments and disputes. How to Track and Recover Retention Systematically? Contractors often overlook retention money rather than treating it as a closeout task. Retention recovery is a whole process problem. Record retention at the point of billing Every RA bill should capture the certified amount, the retention percentage applied, the amount withheld, and the running cumulative retention balance. This is supposed to be the part of the billing record itself, rather than being a separate column that a person updates manually by the end of the month. Set calendar triggers at practical completion The moment the completion certificate is issued, two dates must be locked into the system: The date on which the first tranche of retention becomes claimable The date on which the defects liability period ends and the second tranche becomes claimable These need to be active reminders, not passive notes. Someone should receive a notification 30 days before each date so they have time to prepare the claim, contact the client, and allow for the client&#8217;s internal approval cycle. Submit a formal written claim every time It is necessary to submit a formal written claim every time in a project. The claim must have a few necessary things like contract retention clause references, the name of the project, the completion date, the full per-bill retention history, the cumulative balance, if there&#8217;s any amount already released, and the net amount that is currently due. It is a straightforward formal document that makes everything organized and makes the process easier. Manage the defects liability period actively Consider data loss prevention as a life project phase rather than a waiting period. You need to mention each defect the client reports along with the dates it was raised on and it was resolved on. Then the DLP ends and the final retention claim goes in. A clear report has been submitted to a client which removes any kind of dispute. There are platforms like Onsite that help in connecting billing records, milestone data, and project documentation in a single dashboard. Then the platform tracks retention withheld on every RA bill . The cumulative balance is always visible and currently withhold manual reconstruction. It becomes easier for the accounts team to see retention positions on every completed project. Retention in Subcontractor Contracts: Where Back-to-Back Terms Break Down A principal contractor is not only dealing with retention in one way from client. That person is also withholding the amount of subcontractors, and managing both sides poorly can affect the relationships and cause disputes. It can also create cash flow problems on both ends. The standard approach is to mirror the main contractor retention terms into each subcontractor. For example, if a client is holding 10% of the amount from the principal contractor, the principal contractor withholds the 10% of the amount from each subcontractor. This creates a clean pass-through, but the practice breaks down in two ways. Timing mismatches A plastering subcontractor may complete their work months before the project finishes. From their perspective, the defects liability period should begin once their work is completed and accepted. In many contracts, however, the DLP is tied to the overall project completion date. This means retention is held until the entire project closes, not when the subcontractorâ€™s scope ends. The subcontractor expects release based on their own completion, while the principal holds it longer. This difference in expectation often leads to disputes. Lost payable tracking In a single project, there are often five or more subcontractors that are active. The principal contractor gets too focused on recovering the retention from the client at closure that he forgets that the amount is owed to each subcontractor and it is sitting in a separate file. It is often overlooked and gets delayed, which is why disputes happen. The best way is to record retention obligations clearly in the original work order and to track each subcontractor&#8217;s DLP separately based on scope completion date. Make Retention Recovery a Non-Negotiable Closeout Step Retention money is not extra money. It is an earned income that had been guaranteed to a contractor at the very beginning of the project. The only reason that a contractor may lose it is because of carelessness and no organization. The fix is structural. At every project closeout, the checklist should include retention claim submission as a mandatory step, along with every detail like a name of the owner, a claim submission date, and a follow-up date. The defect liability period end date should also be in the project calendar from the day the completion certificate is signed. Even the billing history should be organized and not reconstructed at the end. FAQs What is the standard retention percentage in Indian construction contracts? The retention percentage in Indian construction contracts typically falls between five and ten percent of each certified RA bill. Government contracts under CPWD or PWD frameworks often fix it at five percent. Private contracts vary by client and negotiation. Some clients apply separate retention rates to material and labour components. The percentage is defined in the contract and applies uniformly across all RA bills unless a cap clause limits the total retention once it reaches a specified amount. Contractors should verify the exact clause before signing and ensure the rate applied during billing matches what the contract specifies. When does retention money get released in Indian construction projects? Most Indian construction contracts release retention in two tranches. The first tranche, typically 50 percent of the total accumulated retention, becomes payable when the client issues the practical completion certificate. The second tranche is released after the defects liability period closes and the contractor has resolved all reported defects. The DLP in Indian contracts commonly runs six to twelve months from the practical completion date. Contractors must submit a formal written claim to trigger each release. The client will not process payment without receiving a proper notice citing the contract clause and the amounts due. What should a formal retention claim letter include? A formal retention claim should include the contract name and number, the relevant retention clause reference, the practical completion certificate date, a full list of RA bills with the retention deducted on each, the cumulative total withheld, any amounts already released, the net amount now due, and the contractor&#8217;s bank details for payment. The letter should also reference the DLP end date when claiming the second tranche, along with a brief record of any defects raised and resolved during the period. This documentation gives the client everything needed to process payment internally without asking for follow-up information. Can a client make deductions from retention money before releasing it? Yes, but only for reasons the contract explicitly permits. Common valid deductions include the cost of fixing defects the contractor did not address during the DLP, liquidated damages for delays not covered by an approved extension of time, and amounts for any unresolved financial claims the client holds against the contractor. Deductions the contract does not authorize are not valid and can be disputed in writing. Contractors should always request a detailed deduction statement before accepting any retention release that is less than the full balance. An undocumented deduction is not a deduction the contractor is obligated to accept. Why do contractors lose retention even when no dispute exists? The most common reason is that no one submits a formal claim on time. Retention does not release automatically. The client has no obligation to remind the contractor when the DLP ends or when the first tranche becomes claimable. When a project closes and the team moves to the next assignment, the retention tracking responsibility falls through the gap between project and finance teams. The accounts team does not have the DLP end date in their calendar. The project manager is on a different site. Months pass, and the retention balance sits uncollected because no one filed a claim. How should subcontractor retention be structured in a back-to-back contract? Subcontractor retention should mirror the main contract retention percentage wherever possible. The subcontract work order should specify the retention rate, the release trigger for the first tranche, and the DLP applicable to the subcontractor&#8217;s specific scope. Where a subcontractor finishes their scope significantly before the overall project completion date, it is good practice to define a scope-specific DLP start date in the work order rather than tying it to the overall project completion. This avoids disputes at closeout and keeps the back-to-back cash flow structure intact. Principal contractors should track retention payable to each sub separately. How does construction management software help recover retention money? Construction management software tracks the retention deduction on every RA bill as part of the billing record. The cumulative retention balance for each project is always current and visible without manual reconciliation. The platform can also link milestone dates like practical completion and DLP end dates to notification triggers that alert the accounts team when a claim becomes due. This removes the single biggest failure point in retention recovery, which is the gap between project closeout and claim submission. When the billing history, milestone records, and retention balance all live in one system, the claim preparation takes minutes rather than days. What is the defects liability period and how does it affect retention release? The defects liability period is the phase after practical completion during which the contractor remains responsible for fixing any defects that appear in the completed work. The client retains the second tranche of retention during this period as a financial guarantee. If the contractor addresses all defects reported during the DLP, the client must release the remaining retention once the period expires. If defects go unaddressed, the client may appoint another contractor to carry out the remedial work and deduct that cost from the retention balance. A well-documented defect resolution record throughout the DLP is the strongest support for a clean final retention claim. Itâ€™s time to move beyond manual chaos and take full control of your labor operations. Also Read: Why Onsite is Indiaâ€™s No.1 Construction Management Software FAQs 1. What is a digital workforce management system in construction? A digital workforce management system helps contractors track labour across sites, record attendance and organize tasks without paperwork. It gives a clear picture of workforce activity every day. 2. How does project tracking software help save time and money? Project tracking software cuts down the time spent collecting attendance, verifying work hours and updating records by hand. It keeps information organised and reduces delays that affect cost. 3. Can this system support many projects running at the same time? Yes. It keeps data from every location in one place. Contractors can monitor labour movement, check attendance and stay updated even when crews work on different projects. 4. Does construction project management software improve accountability? Yes. It creates a visible record of the work done on site. Supervisors can check who is present, how long they worked and what tasks were completed, which reduces confusion. 5. Will using a digital workforce management system reduce manual errors? Yes. Automating labour data eliminates mistakes caused by handwritten notes or late entries. It also helps prevent attendance disputes. 6. Is this solution suitable for both small and large contractors? Yes. It works for small groups working on one site and also for larger teams spread across many projects. The system adapts to the scale of work without extra effort.
